items.title: UPDATE: Three oil supertankers have crossed the Strait of Hormuz today: one Greek owned, and two Chinese. In total, ~6 million barrels. (The Chinese h...
items.description_html:
UPDATE: Three oil supertankers have crossed the Strait of Hormuz today: one Greek owned, and two Chinese. In total, ~6 million barrels.

(The Chinese haven’t yet completed the cross, but they are almost out. The Greek has exited completely). All followed the new shipping lanes.


Javier Blas: As the Islamabad talks are about to start, some activity — at least! — in the Strait of Hormuz.

A couple of Chinese oil supertanker are about to enter the strait via the new shipping lanes. The Cospearl Lake and the He Rong Hai carry together ~4 million of Iraqi and Saudi oil.

items.title: Interesting update on Pennar Industries Company approved issuance of convertible equity warrants worth ~50.4 Cr to promoter entity - Approved issuance...
items.description_html:
Interesting update on Pennar Industries

Company approved issuance of convertible equity warrants worth ~50.4 Cr to promoter entity

- Approved issuance of 30L convertible warrants
- Issue price ~168 per warrant
- Total capital infusion ~50.4 Cr

Shareholding expected to increase from ~15.6% to ~17.4% post conversion

Valuations are no doubt cheap, but there are issues in execution from last few quarters
- PEB growth remained softer than expected despite strong order book visibility
- Working capital continues to remain elevated at ~76 days

- Management reiterated double digit REV & PAT growth guidance for FY26
- All 5 verticals expected to deliver double digit growth
- Labor shortage issue largely resolved & not expected to recur

Key things to monitor going forward
- PEB India order book conversion
- US order book execution
- Telco ramp-up pace over next few quarters
- Working capital improvement trajectory
- PAT margin expansion towards ~5% zone
- Consistency in delivery of 20% PAT growth guidance

Next 2 quarters execution especially in PEB & Telco scaling will be critical to validate guidance credibility

items.description_html:
Interesting Analysis on JM Financials⏬

SOTP valuation on conservative basis + segment overview
(covering Corporate Advisory, Wealth, AMC, Private Markets, Affordable Housing).

Corporate Advisory & Capital Markets remains the core value driver

Wealth Management expected to scale meaningfully
Expansion in distribution + PMS + broking + advisory platform
WM PAT CAGR guided at ~20% till FY28
Asset Management currently in investment phase

Private Markets business emerging as a strong optionality
Focus on private credit, syndication, co-investments, & real estate opportunities

Affordable Housing business building a granular secured loan book
AUM expected to grow ~25%
RoAs guided around ~3%
Potential listing planned by FY28/29

Company is transitioning from a pure capital markets player to a multiengine financial platform with growth coming from Wealth, Alternatives, AMC & Lending businesses over the next few years

src : DAM Capital

items.title: Iran has not ratified UNCLOS & neither has the U.S. ! UNCLOS: UN Convention on the Law of the Sea
items.description_html:
Iran has not ratified UNCLOS & neither has the U.S. !

UNCLOS: UN Convention on the Law of the Sea


Husain Haqqani: The Strait of Hormuz is an international strait. Under UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (which Iran has not ratified) all ships & aircraft enjoy freedom of navigation & overflight. No state can suspend this right. Rights are not subject to negotiation & renegotiation.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell summoned Wall Street leaders to give them an urgent warning.

An artificial intelligence tool from Anthropic PBC - Mythos - marks the beginning of a new era of cybersecurity.

What is Mythos?

Claude Mythos Preview is a general purpose AI model that Anthropic says significantly outperforms prior offerings on a range of benchmarks, including for coding and reasoning. The company says it’s so powerful that it has decided not to release it to the public. 

Here's an explainer
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-10/mythos-why-anthropic-s-new-ai-has-officials-worried

items.title: I spoke to a few editors before publishing the manuscript, and while they suggested quite radical changes, neither had experience in the CDMO space. I...
items.description_html:
I spoke to a few editors before publishing the manuscript, and while they suggested quite radical changes, neither had experience in the CDMO space. I wasn’t comfortable with my natural tone and thinking being reshaped in ways that might dilute the intent.

I knew it wasn’t perfect, and I was prepared to fail. I certainly didn’t expect it to reach so many readers ... and it's still growing. I wrote it to challenge thinking, not just to convey ideas. I’m genuinely grateful to everyone who sat with it long enough for it to click. I hope I can do even better next time.

The next book, whenever it lands, will be simpler, more accessible, and much wider in scope. It’s shaping up well, and I’m pleased with the context and case studies coming together. It's been 18 months in the making ... some more pain left.

I’ve been very fortunate over the decades, and I’m deeply grateful for it all ... 🙏

items.title: RT ValueEquity: Was reading the latest Investor Note by 2point2 Capital's @amitmantri , few things that really resonated with the way I think about di...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
Was reading the latest Investor Note by 2point2 Capital's @amitmantri , few things that really resonated with the way I think about differentiated insights in the world of AI

One thing that AI has done is democratized research for anyone who is willing to read and learn , but the issue is what most people don't understand yet is, more data does not mean more insight

In fact , it can mean quite the opposite , I have seen AI in some ways make investors lazy on core fundamental investing processes which include reading long annual reports , credit ratings , going on ground , creating mechanism for channel checks , understanding the cycle of the business

Now you see most people just trying to engineer prompts to shorten the research cycle , and focus on data gathering rather than diffrentiated insights

we need to realize that comes at the cost of the depth that actually derives the conviction that helps us to actually own businesses through an earnings cycle , shortening the research cycle will lead to no depth in research , especially when times are tough , you can build the best predictable model with huge DCF's with Claude predicting earnings to the last decimal , but what will you do when the stock falls 20% in a day , and falls 5% the next day ? None of the data points that seemed to provide the illusion of complete research will help you survive that , but just core depth of research , on ground insights and conviction will , none of which can be extracted from AI

Use AI that enables you to improve functionality of research , but trying to use it to build a case for conviction and making AI as a large part of the investment thesis wouldn't be a good idea in my opinion

On the contrary , I feel the Lazy investor that uses AI tools optimally to help rather than fully ideate has a bigger edge , as this market is not short of fancy data points or models it is short of conviction and the ability to back your thesis when times are tough

Use the powerful AI tools to help your individual ideation process and increase efficiencies , not to be dependent for things that are the most powerful in the human mind , the ability to think , do not surrender that !

items.title: Partly agree and strongly disagree with Nikhil Sir Part 1 - New Age/AI stocks AI & New Age stocks as % of total market cap US - 14% in CY16 to 40% in ...
items.description_html:
Partly agree and strongly disagree with Nikhil Sir

Part 1 - New Age/AI stocks

AI & New Age stocks as % of total market cap

US - 14% in CY16 to 40% in Oct 25
China - 4% in CY16 to 15% in Oct 25
India - 0% in CY16 to 2% in Oct 25

Src - MOSL, Moneycontrol

One of the reasons for FII outflow - AI and New age is the flavor of the season, and India has a lower percentage

Part 2 - Rupee Depreciation

Rupee depreciated ~10% at its peak against the USD (right now 8.5%), thanks to RBI interventions

FIIs convert $ to ₹ when they invest in India, and every 1 Rs depreciation against the $ means lesser returns for FIIs when they convert back their ₹ into $ - this may prompt them to sell more

Example - Assume $4 invested at 1$ = 75₹ rate, so 4$ means ₹300. Assuming ₹ 300 becomes ₹360, and they want to sell, but that time 1$ = ₹90, so again they get only 4$ back, if 1$ = 50 ₹, then they get 6$ (so ₹ appreciation helps them)

Obviously, RBI has taken a lot of steps here to curb the manipulation/speculation in the USD/INR trades, and they will do more, I am sure!

Part 3 - Taxation

FIIs sold ₹1.6 lakh crores in 2025 and ₹1.1 lakh crores in just the first 3 months of 2026

Rs 2.7 Lakh cr of investments exited India in just 15 months

If SIP Book hadn't supported the DIIs, our markets would have been under the ground

Partially Agreed, Govt. should do something to reward long-term patient capital here for sure -

a - LTCG/STCG tax reduction + Capital Gains Tax Stability Framework - Commit to a 5–10 year tax roadmap for equities (no surprises).
b - Conversation with FIIs to bring them back (cut down on the operational hassles and taxation issues) c - Introduce Tax deductions for equity SIPs (like 80C-lite category)
d- Position India as “China+1 capital market” or play on the Geopolitical neutral image
e - Push settlement of trades/investments in INR globally - Reduces FX risk dependency
f - Create a Sovereign Wealth fund via LIC and other big players in the war Footing
g - Build more on Mutual Fund Sahi hain Campaign - More Campaigns can be created, such as Equity investments Sahi hain, har mahine Stock Investment Yojana, and so on

Part 4 - The Mean Reversion/Valuation Catchup Game

5 year EPS growth is 16%, and Sensex CAGR is 8% - Never happened before, the gap of 8% is the highest ever, and we all know that Earnings growth = Market growth (~100% correlation)

I feel FIIs are acting like hot money and just trying to speculate with their money (China, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, LATAM)

Good news and good prices never come together

If you are a long-term equity/Mutual fund investor in India, you should have been topping up money continuously during the last 18 months of turbulence (or just staying put)

Even now, there is amazing value in some pockets of the market

Study double the number of companies and spend time on the valuations and fundamentals, lots of mispriced bets in the market

The seeds of a rally are sown in corrective phases of the market! All the best :)


Nithin Kamath: Asked someone from the industry whether foreign investors are still interested in allocating to India. The TLDR:

Interest has pretty much died out. India is seen as geopolitically exposed, especially to an oil shock. There are no real AI plays. Valuations are rich. And the rupee

items.title: Brad Setser is pleased to see the IMF conceding that intervention by a country in its currency and external accounts “can systematically generate rea...
items.description_html:
Brad Setser is pleased to see the IMF conceding that intervention by a country in its currency and external accounts “can systematically generate real exchange rate depreciation and raise current account balances”.

He is being quite polite. We have known for decades that a country’s external imbalances must always be perfectly consistent with its internal imbalances, one implication of which is that if a country’s industrial policies result in the creation of persistent domestic imbalances, it can only maintain those policies to the extent that it controls its external account in ways that allow it to externalize these domestic imbalances.

So, for example, if a country’s financial system is designed mainly to take household and business saving and direct this saving into approved investment at very low interest rates, it has to control its capital account to prevent domestic saving from flowing abroad.

Or, as another example, if a country’s industrial policies require transfers from the household sector to subsidize manufacturing, this is likely to lead to persistent trade surpluses, in which case the country must intervene in the currency to prevent its appreciation from reversing those transfers. An appreciating currency reverses these transfers by effectively subsidizing households (households are net importers) and taxing producers of tradable goods.

This is nothing new. Keynes showed many decades ago that with a few, fairly obvious exceptions (when trade partners – e.g. rapidly growing developing countries, Europe and Japan after WW2, etc. – need to import capital for domestic investment purposes), persistent manufacturing surpluses are prima facie evidence of domestic interventions whose domestic costs are being exported to trade partners.

Without some form of intervention in the currency or the trade and capital accounts, in other words, policies that lead to domestic imbalances are much harder to maintain.


Brad Setser: I also take a bit of satisfaction in this conclusion; it explains why I have been systematically tracking official asset accumulation for close to 20 years!

2/

items.title: When a company in my watchlist goes from tracking to initiating , always it has to win the spot , treat it like this You are a capital allocator looki...
items.description_html:
When a company in my watchlist goes from tracking to initiating , always it has to win the spot , treat it like this

You are a capital allocator looking to allocate in the best business so you can generate a higher risk adjust return than the market , there cannot be any soft spots or biases , the company has to prove the need to be there , use the edge of being an investor in your favor , don't fall for stories /narratives or themes

You are a capital allocator looking to make returns as a shareholder with good entry valuations , rest all is later

items.description_html:
Most of us in India know about the Ganges dolphin found in the Ganga, Brahmaputra and their tributaries.

Lesser-known is its western cousin, the Indus dolphin. This dolphin is today found mostly in the Indus river basin in Pakistan. In India, it is solely found in the Beas river in Punjab, a tributary of the Indus.

@GitanjaliKanwar and Basanta Rajkumar write about how conservation reserves, citizen science, and habitat protection give the Indus River dolphin a fighting chance in India.

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/wildlife-biodiversity/angel-of-the-beas

items.description_html:
RT CNBC-TV18
‘It’s not about what you like, you make what sells.’

On Young Turks Reloaded with Shereen Bhan, Lahori Zeera’s Nikhil Doda bottles up a story that’s equal parts nostalgia and nerve - from never sipping a cola to crafting a ₹700 crore desi beverage powerhouse.

He takes us back to the late ’90s, when his father ran a small soft drinks business.
‘He was pretty excited… said it’s a good space to be in if you can differentiate’

items.title: Gold is reshaping the global financial system: Central bank gold holdings surpassed valuation-adjusted US Dollar reserve assets for the first time on ...
items.description_html:
Gold is reshaping the global financial system:

Central bank gold holdings surpassed valuation-adjusted US Dollar reserve assets for the first time on record.

Adjusted USD reserves remove the interest earned on holdings like US Treasuries, showing only how much central banks are actively adding to their Dollar reserves.

Official gold reserve assets are up to a record $3.87 trillion, ~$140 billion above valuation-adjusted US Dollar reserve assets, at $3.73 trillion.

Since 2022, gold reserve assets have TRIPLED, driven by a record central bank accumulation and surging prices.

At the same time, USD reserve assets have declined -$300 billion.

The trend accelerated after the seizure of Russian assets in 2022, new sanctions, and growing geopolitical uncertainty.

World central banks are accumulating gold at an unprecedented pace.

items.title: I go running at Shree Shiv Chhatrapati Sports Complex (Balewadi Stadium) near Orchid Hotel in Pune I see many old people using the body weight equipme...
items.description_html:
I go running at Shree Shiv Chhatrapati Sports Complex (Balewadi Stadium) near Orchid Hotel in Pune

I see many old people using the body weight equipments

In fact, a group of oldies who come there for walking have created their own club and their own shirts that they wear every day; Some even get their grandchildren

Individuals/Couples come for running

Children come for swimming, running, badminton, cricket, hockey, skating, and many more sports

Folks come and sit there to find peace; Some even do Yoga

Sports Training classes are also carried out

This is the power of great public spaces. Going out should not be just eating food and roaming in Malls

We need more public spaces, parks, and sports infrastructure, or else we are just going to create crores of patients for the hospital ecosystem!

Just like a Car needs maintenance, our bodies need maintenance by daily exercise and activity too!

items.title: US private equity firms have massive exposure to software: A near-record 49% of all private equity deals now target software and technology services c...
items.description_html:
US private equity firms have massive exposure to software:

A near-record 49% of all private equity deals now target software and technology services companies.

The percentage has DOUBLED over the last 15 years.

This comes as private market managers poured hundreds of billions of dollars into software firms over this period, betting that software-as-a-service (SaaS) business models would produce strong growth and consistent cash generation.

By comparison, healthcare represents 14% of PE deals, less than a third of the size of software exposure.

The private equity industry has never been this exposed to a single sector.

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: 1900's, Kyd street, Calcutta. (Kolkata). Kyd Street, now officially known as Dr. Md. Ishaque Road, is a historic British-era str...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
1900's, Kyd street, Calcutta. (Kolkata). Kyd Street, now officially known as Dr. Md. Ishaque Road, is a historic British-era street located near Park Street in central Kolkata. Renowned for its legacy buildings, it was historically prominent for holding the Apcar Bungalow and represents the colonial architectural landscape of the 19th/early 20th century, located in the heart of the city's, now, bustling district.

The Apcar Bungalow, located at 14 Kyd Street (now Dr. Md. Ishaque Road) in Kolkata, is a significant, yet endangered, piece of colonial-era heritage. Associated with the wealthy Armenian-Indian Apcar family, who were prominent merchants and shipowners in the 19th-century Calcutta, the building represents the city's lost architectural legacy amidst modern urban development.

It was once a hub for elite colonial residents, reflecting the influence of the Armenian community in Kolkata's trade. The Apcar family was famous for the Apcar Line (shipping company). #Kolkata #street #heritage #history @KanchanGupta

items.title: E-commerce has never been bigger in the US: E-commerce now reflects a record 16.6% of total US retail sales. Put simply, nearly $1 out of every $6 spe...
items.description_html:
E-commerce has never been bigger in the US:

E-commerce now reflects a record 16.6% of total US retail sales.

Put simply, nearly $1 out of every $6 spent in US retail is now online.

By comparison, it was just 0.8% in Q1 2000, meaning activity has surged by over 20x since 2000.

In Q4 2025, online sales surged to a record $365.2 billion.

As a result, total US e-commerce sales for the full year 2025 were estimated at a record $1.23 trillion, up +5.4% from 2024.

Online shopping is replacing brick-and-mortar.

items.title: BREAKING: US M2 money supply jumped +4.8% YoY in February, to a record $22.6 trillion, marking the 24th consecutive monthly increase. Money supply is ...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: US M2 money supply jumped +4.8% YoY in February, to a record $22.6 trillion, marking the 24th consecutive monthly increase.

Money supply is now ~$700 billion above the March 2022 peak.

Since the 2020 pandemic, M2 has surged +$7.1 trillion, or roughly +$1.2 trillion per year.

Since 2000, money in circulation has grown at an average annual rate of +6.2%.

The US Dollar is losing purchasing power at a historic pace.

items.title: Used car prices are skyrocketing: Wholesale used vehicle prices rose +4.2% MoM and +5.7% YoY in March, to $20,102, the highest since mid-2023. This al...
items.description_html:
Used car prices are skyrocketing:

Wholesale used vehicle prices rose +4.2% MoM and +5.7% YoY in March, to $20,102, the highest since mid-2023.

This also marks the largest YoY increase since the 2020 pandemic price spike.

EVs led the surge at +8.0% YoY, to $28,471, the highest since February 2024.

This was followed by non-EV, which jumped +6.0% YoY, to the highest level since October 2023.

Wholesale used vehicle prices are now +$6,500, or +48%, above the pre-pandemic levels seen in 2019.

Used-car price pressure is building again.

items.title: US crude oil exports are skyrocketing: US Gulf Coast crude exports are on track to average a record 4.90 million barrels a day in April based on curre...
items.description_html:
US crude oil exports are skyrocketing:

US Gulf Coast crude exports are on track to average a record 4.90 million barrels a day in April based on current loadings.

That would be up +23%, or +930,000 barrels per day since March, and up +30%, or +1.12 million barrels per day since February.

At the current pace, May exports will exceed 5.0 million barrels a day for the first time in history.

This comes as Asian buyers are securing US cargoes to offset the loss of Middle Eastern supply while flows from the Strait of Hormuz remain significantly limited.

For May alone, 28 supertankers are already lined up to carry US crude, compared to 5 typically booked at this point in a month.

Global demand for American oil has never been higher.

items.title: BREAKING: The US personal savings rate dropped -0.5 percentage points in February, to 4.0%, the 2nd-lowest since November 2022. Excluding 2022, saving...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The US personal savings rate dropped -0.5 percentage points in February, to 4.0%, the 2nd-lowest since November 2022.

Excluding 2022, savings rates are now at their lowest levels since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

In 2022, elevated inflation eroded purchasing power, forcing American households to spend more on essentials.

The savings rate has declined -1.5 percentage points since April 2025.

Furthermore, real disposable income growth has weakened, limiting the ability of consumers to rebuild savings.

By comparison, the last 5-year's average was 6.5%, while the 5-year pre-pandemic average stood at 6.0%.

Households are struggling to save money.

items.description_html:
US inflation rose to 3.3% in March on Middle East energy shock
US inflation rose to 3.3 per cent in March, the highest since May 2024, as the surge in energy prices triggered by the Iran war ricocheted across the world’s biggest economy. @FT

items.title: Hedge funds are unwinding their short exposure: Hedge fund short positions in US macro products, including index futures and ETFs, are down -1.0 perce...
items.description_html:
Hedge funds are unwinding their short exposure:

Hedge fund short positions in US macro products, including index futures and ETFs, are down -1.0 percentage point over the last 2 days, to 11.2%, the lowest since the beginning of March.

This comes as US-listed ETF shorts fell -7.4% during Wednesday’s session, the largest 1-day drop since August 2020.

This also marks the largest single-day short covering over the last 10 years, led by large-cap stocks.

Still, short positioning in US equity macro products remains 4 percentage points above the 2024 low.

Over the last 5 years, short exposure has only been higher in 3% of cases.

Hedge funds still have a massive amount of short exposure to unwind.

items.title: The US stock market is seeing a historic recovery: The S&P 500 has finished green for 7 consecutive trading sessions, the longest streak since October...
items.description_html:
The US stock market is seeing a historic recovery:

The S&P 500 has finished green for 7 consecutive trading sessions, the longest streak since October 2025.

The index has rallied +7.6% over this period, recovering nearly the entire war decline.

A similar 7-day stretch has also been recorded by the Nasdaq Composite, the longest since August 2025.

Since the 1950s, the market has seen a similar streak with at least a +7.0% gain only 9 other times, per Carson Investment Research.

Following this, the S&P 500 has been higher in 8 of those instances over the next month, with an average return of +4.4%.

Over the following 3 months, the market has been up in 7 instances and has gained +10.2% on average.

History says market momentum is set to continue.

items.title: How George Fernandes (even if he didn’t intend to) became the father of the Indian Stock market? Mr Ashok Damani says that George Fernandes had state...
items.description_html:
How George Fernandes (even if he didn’t intend to) became the father of the Indian Stock market?

Mr Ashok Damani says that George Fernandes had stated in one of his speeches that all MNCs in India would have to dilute their stake to less than 50% if they wanted to continue operating in India.

Some companies, like Coca-Cola, packed their bags and left India for 30 years. But many other MNCs like Nestle, Colgate, and Glaxo decided to stay and got listed in India.

As a result, the investible universe suddenly expanded. People like the Late Chandrakant Sampat and my father, Ramesh Damani, who understood the value of these franchises, flocked to these stocks.

Till 1992, when liberation happened, this was the pool of stocks that we had. This also created a pool of investors who knew how to judge good stocks and what to look for in the years to come.

So when the economic reforms happened in 1992, we were ready. The investing public was ready for the big boom. In many ways, George Fernandes (even if he didn’t intend to) became the father of the Indian Stock market - what we call the law of unintended consequences!

His forcing companies to dilute their stake created an investible universe, which created a set of smart investors, which helped us in 1992.

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

items.title: RT The Indian Express: War Over Bauxite: Tribal-Police Clash In Rayagada Village Road Project, Mahua Moitra Slams President. Armed with traditional ax...
items.description_html:
RT The Indian Express
War Over Bauxite: Tribal-Police Clash In Rayagada Village Road Project, Mahua Moitra Slams President.

Armed with traditional axes and stones, tribal villagers in Odisha’s Rayagada engaged in a violent multi-hour standoff with police to block a road project linked to a major bauxite mine.

The clash, which left senior officers critically injured and forced police to resort to tear gas, marks a deadly escalation in the years-long resistance against mining in the ecologically sensitive Sijimali hills.

While the administration has imposed Section 163 to restore order, the local community vows that their land will not be sacrificed for corporate bauxite extraction.

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: Rainmatter started in 2016, with a few of us doubling up on our day jobs and trying to help startups that were trying to expand Indi...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
Rainmatter started in 2016, with a few of us doubling up on our day jobs and trying to help startups that were trying to expand India’s capital markets ecosystem. Nine years later, it has grown into something far bigger than we ever imagined.

So far, we’ve invested over ₹1,500 crore across 160+ startups spanning fintech, climate, health, media, and deep tech. We’ve also earmarked 10% of everything Zerodha earns to invest in startups, and another 10% for the social sector through the @RainmatterOrg.

The thesis has evolved from just expanding the capital markets, but the thread running through it is simple. As a country, we need to own more of what we consume. Sovereignty, in the truest sense.

We’re not a typical VC. We don’t take board seats, and we’re not in this for quick exits. We’re not interested in forcing founders into short-term decisions just so we can make money in five or six years.

The simple reality is that building a good business is hard. Building one that is genuinely useful, scalable, and profitable is even harder when investors are pushing you to speedrun success and sustainability.

That kind of pressure usually leads to shortcuts. And shortcuts, more often than not, come at the consumer’s expense.

So our approach has been simple: be patient, back founders for the long term, and help them build the business the right way.

That, more than anything else, is the heart of @Rainmatterin.

items.title: RT ValueEquity: I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company Things like 1) How frequently the company talks t...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company

Things like
1) How frequently the company talks to the media
2) How often to KMP trade in and out of the stock
3) Does the company spend on digital presence and PR
4) Does hiring growth match guidance for company growth ( Linkedin/Naukri )
5)Suppliers and vendors reputation of the company, credit policy , payment terms , sales rep aggression
6) Does the company keep doing webinars with PMS managers to justify the business
7) Are they ecosystem focused or product focused , to understand the bigger picture with data like and acquisition for further growth close to greenfield plots
8) Nature of Clients with tenure mapping

These are some of the things that we must look into other than the typical B/S and Cash flow analysis also , the business is a moving picture with many parts , to understand the whole picture we must broaden the vision

@ishmohit1

items.description_html:
RT Siddharth Misra
The "white collar" middle was NEVER the knowledge class it was a translation class. AI is collapsing the friction gap they were paid to fill.
Wrote about ALL THIS, also where that leaves the Conduit class (and what survives): http://thegreyswan.substack.com/p/the-death-of-the-in-between

items.title: RT menaka doshi: I’ve spent the last month asking women in India about the size of their vanity kits. Ratna, 22, lives in a Mumbai shanty town and cl...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
I’ve spent the last month asking women in India about the size of their vanity kits.

Ratna, 22, lives in a Mumbai shanty town and cleans offices. Two years ago, her vanity bag had just lipstick and kajal (kohl). Now it includes moisturizer and eyeliner.

Radhika, 30, works a desk job at a multinational corporation in Bengaluru. Her vanity kit has grown from two items to seven including sunscreen, lip balm, eyeshadow and blush.

Nalini, 45, used one cream brand all through her youth — now she has enough brands to fill a large shelf.

There’s no business like the beauty business in India right now.

items.title: The rise of mobile phones powered access to the internet in India. In the ten years from 2014 to 2024, India added 30 million wired internet connectio...
items.description_html:
The rise of mobile phones powered access to the internet in India. In the ten years from 2014 to 2024, India added 30 million wired internet connections. In the same time, India added nearly 900 million mobile broadband connections.

Household surveys point to a similar expansion of household-level internet access; only 2% of homes in India had internet access in 2009, but once mobile broadband entered the space, the pace of access accelerated. By 2023, this had increased to 76% of households.

Alongside household access, the usage of the internet has grown too. As of 2023, nearly six in ten Indians had used the internet at least once in the last three months, according to a nationally representative survey.

Internet usage is higher among more urban, better educated and richer Indians, and more among men than women. Internet usage is also rising steadily - the share of Indian teenagers using the internet is double that of Indians above the age of 60 using it.

How does internet access in India look across different income and education levels? Read @akwaghmare’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/comm-tech/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Mobile #Phone #Internet #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Vikash Jain – CLSA After 18 months of being bearish, we turn constructive on Indian stocks We may have surpassed the “maximum pain”...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Vikash Jain – CLSA

After 18 months of being bearish, we turn constructive on Indian stocks
We may have surpassed the “maximum pain” point regarding both the Iran conflict and its impact on the market. With sentiment reaching extreme bearishness and valuations (on both an absolute and relative basis) trading below 10-year averages, we argue that risk-reward profile for Indian equities has finally balanced after 18 months of being unfavourable. Downside protection strategy helped our portfolio to outperform Nifty by 6ppt in last quarter. We now move toward capturing upside and make a slew of changes to our portfolio.

Portfolio Changes
We exit ITC in favour of Varun Beverages and from Bajaj Auto to Mahindra & Mahindra
We expect a better post-war set-up for Vedanta over Ultratech Cement.
We see L&T as a post-war recovery play and bring it in place of the defensive NTPC
We see better risk-reward in Bajaj Finance than IndusInd Bank
We raise O-WT on financials and cut IT to U-WT by swapping Tech Mahindra for HDFC Bank
We continue to hold ICICI Bank, SBI, ONGC, Tata Motors (CV + PV), Infosys, DMart, Eternal and Godrej Properties in our portfolio.
@_prashantnair @Nigel__DSouza @Reematendulkar
@CNBCTV18News @CNBCTV18Live

items.title: God Bless Promoters like Devi Shetty - From Their FY24 Annual Report - "Every day I see between 60 to 100 heart patients in my OPD. Most of them come ...
items.description_html:
God Bless Promoters like Devi Shetty -

From Their FY24 Annual Report -

"Every day I see between 60 to 100 heart patients in my OPD. Most of them come from working-class and poor families. When I tell the mother of a child with a hole in the heart that the surgery costs 2 or 3 lakh rupees, which she does not have, she breaks down crying. It is the most humiliating experience for any parent to learn the price of their child’s life, and I see this many times a day. After this, she touches my feet and begs me to save her child’s life, saying that she can somehow arrange 1 lakh rupees to save her child.

This young mother inspired me to come up with a health insurance plan in which 1 lakh rupees can instead pay for many years of premiums that can cover the cost of surgeries, including liver, kidney, and heart transplants, for her entire family." Narayana Health offers a health insurance plan called Aditi that costs Rs 10,000 per year for a family.

The plan offers up to Rs 1 crore for surgeries, and Rs 5 lakh for general hospital stays within Narayana Health's network of hospitals.

Hope this plan sees more success. In India, a very big % is just one hospitalization away from poverty! Sad but true!

no stock reco


Nithin Kamath: I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured out how to do open heart surgery for a few hundred dollars when the same procedure costs a bomb in the US. Narayana has 18,000 beds across India,

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: I hope those advising the U.S. President are making the following points clear: 1. Ira...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
I hope those advising the U.S. President are making the following points clear:

1. Iran sees itself as having achieved a significant strategic gain. From its perspective, if its terms are not met, there will be no meeting in Islamabad, even at the cost of renewed escalation.

2. Iran is unlikely to reopen the straits without a full ceasefire, which it believes was promised, even under pressure or threats.

3. Tehran has no intention of offering new concessions beyond what has already been discussed with the U.S. It views itself as negotiating from a position of strength, so why concede more?

4. The “Axis of Resistance” operates as an interconnected system. As long as fighting continues in Lebanon, Shiite militias in Iraq and potentially the Houthis, are likely to remain engaged.

Iran does not see itself as having been defeated. It did not seek these negotiations, and it is unrealistic to expect concessions at the table if, in its own assessment, it has not conceded on the battlefield.

items.title: RT Andrew Peretti: Sure - a Tax & STT reduction will help, but where are the blue sky capital market reforms? We need policy mavericks out there willi...
items.description_html:
RT Andrew Peretti
Sure - a Tax & STT reduction will help, but where are the blue sky capital market reforms? We need policy mavericks out there willing to advocate for bold reform for the benefit of the whole market. Can't you be one of them @Nithin0dha

A few of my personal ideas for whatever little it is worth:

1) Liberalised and streamlined regime for foreign retail investors? I'd love to invest, but no thanks to going through the lengthy FPI registration process to make retail size investments. Nothing would boost volumes or goodwill more than this.

2) Allow FPI/MF to intraday trade to promote cash market liquidity. Make cash great again. Why can you trade F&O intraday but not cash as an FPI, for example?

3) Create stock lending pools at FPI/ODI issuers, by allowing OTC stock borrowing/synthetic access. (Complex topic, DM me)

4) Allow gross custodian funding for FPIs and even broker funding for well capitalized intermediaries (...no, not just for failed DVP trades) in order to create capital efficiency for investors.

5) Create an after hours cash market to help overseas/domestics to manage overnight risk and spur round the clock trading? (To the 8-4pm brokers: a couple of hours a week on rotation will be good for you :))

6) Futures - given the focus on the linear market, why not allow futures on every BSE 500 stock or more? (Look at the US, nearly every company has listed options)

7) Local Funds- I have friends running PMS/AIFs. The cost is attractive versus other jurisdictions, but they feel suffocated by the bureaucracy. So much to learn from some of the overseas fund jurisdictions, where private funds can be setup with minimal compliance burden and tax efficiency.

8) Allow unsponsored ADRs/GDRs (perhaps in a trusted jurisdiction, FTA countries?). This would helps create visibility for Indian companies and a wider audience. Full fungibility on all ADRs/GDRs would also help some current companies.

I'll add more as I think of them.

Frankly, fund managers, brokers, should take a lot more interest in these market reform topics: your livelihood depends on it...It seems like people are afraid to put their head about the parapet though.


Nithin Kamath: Asked someone from the industry whether foreign investors are still interested in allocating to India. The TLDR:

Interest has pretty much died out. India is seen as geopolitically exposed, especially to an oil shock. There are no real AI plays. Valuations are rich. And the rupee

items.title: The impact of AI on "white collar" services industry (Consultants, Bankers, Lawyers) is quite visible. And I think it's for the better. For decades, t...
items.description_html:
The impact of AI on "white collar" services industry (Consultants, Bankers, Lawyers) is quite visible. And I think it's for the better.

For decades, the job markets over glorified making beautiful presentations, document comparisons or elaborate 20 sheet excel models - making these some of the most well paid professions. Money attracts talent and thus some of the smarter minds (high entry barriers ensure natural filtration) were doing non consequential work.

On the other hand, material science fields that really lead to progress (Scientists, PHDs, Manufacturing minds) failed to draw same incomes on an average.

AI is not replacing these white collar fields, but shifting focus to what should matter. From the optics of it: detailing, look and feel --> to the outcomes of it. From glorifying long working hours --> to asking for value of these decisions. It is clearly narrowing the team pyramid by eroding value in the mundane and shifting it to quality of judgment, thinking and impact.

I am not throwing a hyperbole here, but check what fraction of M&As have turned out well, how many companies created value after a mega Consulting (MBB) assignment, lawyers made top bills per hour for adding complexity. In short, the big payout in while collar field remainer tied to "completion" of a project and not to the "outcomes" of it.

As for the younger folks who have entered job markets in the eye of AI disruption - you would do well to become champion of embracing AI in your company - this doesn't come so naturally to your experienced seniors, and thus you become valuable. AI doesnt mean less work, it means "smarter hours", and more output. Barter your AI readiness for the experience/ network of your seniors to stay and grow.

In short, I think the wage pyramid will have to readjust and redistribute in the coming years/ decades, and job markets would have to adjust to a new reality.

items.title: RT SOIC Research: This is the second part of our "Finding Moated Business" series, following our previous discussion on INOX INDIA. The focus this tim...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
This is the second part of our "Finding Moated Business" series, following our previous discussion on INOX INDIA.

The focus this time is on another highly intriguing Indian chemical company. It's one of only four such companies globally, which strongly suggests the presence of a significant competitive advantage, or MOAT, in its business model.

In this thread, we will analyze this business in three key steps:
1) Understanding what makes this business MOAT-ed and how.

2) Assessing if this MOAT translates into robust financial performance for the company.

3) Determining if this MOAT's primary function is to simply accumulate cash on the balance sheet at a normalized growth rate, or if it provides the capacity for growth significantly faster than its industry peers.

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: Asked someone from the industry whether foreign investors are still interested in allocating to India. The TLDR: Interest has pretty...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
Asked someone from the industry whether foreign investors are still interested in allocating to India. The TLDR:

Interest has pretty much died out. India is seen as geopolitically exposed, especially to an oil shock. There are no real AI plays. Valuations are rich. And the rupee situation doesn't help.

On top of that, investors who were sitting on gains have taken money off the table and are now looking at markets like Japan, Taiwan, Korea, Europe etc instead.

He also pointed out that our LTCG/STCG structure and the increase in STT have made India less attractive compared to other markets that are seeing inflows.

If we need to attract FPIs back, and we do, fixing this feels like pretty low-hanging fruit.

items.title: G.M.Breweries delivered a strong operational quarter with revenue growth of 22.46% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 895 bps to 25.9%, driven by exce...
items.description_html:
G.M.Breweries delivered a strong operational quarter with revenue growth of 22.46% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 895 bps to 25.9%, driven by excellent operating leverage and cost discipline. The 82% surge in core operational profit (PBT ex-Other Income) demonstrates true business strength despite headline PAT declining 10.6% YoY due to lower other income and higher taxes.

The major red flag is significantly negative operating cash flow of ₹(9,562) Lakh driven by unexplained increases in other assets, though management's 20% dividend increase signals confidence. The stock offers alpha for investors who can see through the reported numbers to the strong operational performance and margin inflection story.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 22.46% YoY to ₹812 Cr in Q4, 18.89% for full year to ₹2,977 Cr

EBITDA margins expanded 895 bps to 25.9% in Q4, 570 bps for full year to 24.2%

Core operational profit (PBT ex-Other Income) surged 82% YoY in Q4

Cost of materials grew only 2.7% YoY in Q4 vs 19.5% net revenue growth

Capacity utilization improved to ~66.3% from 55.74% validating expansion strategy

Dividend increased 20% to ₹9 per share showing management confidence

Company remains debt-free with equity growing 14.9% YoY to ₹1,078 Cr

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow turned significantly negative at ₹(9,562) Lakh vs ₹16,168 Lakh positive in FY25

Other Financial Assets increased ₹10,666 Lakh and Other Current Assets ₹9,876 Lakh causing cash burn

Cash and bank balances declined 87.16% YoY indicating liquidity pressure

Tax expense jumped 78.8% YoY to ₹1,405 Lakh in Q4
Reported PAT declined 10.6% YoY despite strong

operational performance - confusing for investors
Free cash flow negative at ₹(14,114) Lakh for FY26

items.description_html:
Anand Rathi reported strong reported numbers with 29.7% revenue growth and 40.3% PAT growth, exceeding FY26 guidance. However, normalizing for exceptional other income reveals Q4 normalized PAT declined 8.7% YoY and full-year normalized growth of 16.6% missed the 20-25% target.

The company demonstrated strong balance sheet transformation with 447% cash increase and 21.8% debt reduction, but core operational momentum shows signs of slowdown with flat QoQ revenue and elevated employee costs.

Positive Factors

Revenue from Operations grew 29.7% YoY to ₹287.82 Cr in Q4

Reported PAT grew 40.3% YoY to ₹103.45 Cr in Q4
Normalized EBITDA margin expanded 571 bps YoY to 46.5%

Full-year FY26 revenue exceeded guidance by 6.6%

Full-year FY26 PAT exceeded guidance by 5.6%

Cash position increased 447% YoY to ₹240.94 Cr

Total debt reduced 21.8% YoY to ₹17.02 Cr

AUM reached ₹99,008 Cr nearing ₹1,00,000 Cr target

Risk Factors

Normalized PAT declined 8.7% YoY in Q4 masking core operational weakness

Normalized PAT growth of 16.6% YoY missed management's 20-25% long-term target

Employee costs surged 66.6% YoY in Q4 outpacing revenue growth

Relationship Manager additions very slow - only 13 net additions in 9 months

Q4 revenue down 0.6% QoQ indicating sequential slowdown

Deferred tax expense high at 40.7% of total tax in Q4

items.title: RT Takshashila Institution: "If everything is critical, nothing is critical." — Sound familiar? Oil. LPG. Helium. Sulphur. Lithium. Cobalt. Rare Eart...
items.description_html:
RT Takshashila Institution
"If everything is critical, nothing is critical." — Sound familiar?

Oil. LPG. Helium. Sulphur. Lithium. Cobalt. Rare Earths. The list of "critical minerals" keeps growing — but the confusion around them grows faster.
Is it an environmental issue? A national security threat? A China problem? The answer is: all of the above. And that's exactly why it's so hard to make sense of.

Takshashila Institution's Politics & Policy of Critical Minerals cuts through the noise. No geology jargon. No academic rabbit holes. Just the geopolitics, supply chain realities, and policy choices that will define the next decade of global power.

items.title: RT Parimal: In 1966, a B-52 collided with a tanker over Palomares, Spain, dropping 4 hydrogen bombs. 3 were found on land; 1 vanished into the Mediter...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
In 1966, a B-52 collided with a tanker over Palomares, Spain, dropping 4 hydrogen bombs. 3 were found on land; 1 vanished into the Mediterranean. The US Navy spent months searching with no luck.

They called in a mathematician named John Craven, who ignored the expert search grids. Instead, he used Bayes' Theorem.

He asked various salvage experts to bet on different scenarios (e.g., "What is the likelihood the parachute deployed?"). He treated their guesses as Priors. As the submersibles searched areas & found nothing (Evidence), he used the formula to update the probability map.

The bomb was found exactly where the Bayesian map said it would be, an area the expert searchers had previously dismissed as highly unlikely.


vixhaℓ: Most people with “AI/ML” in their bios don’t even know what Bayes theorem is.

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured out how to do open heart surgery for a few hundred dollars when the same procedure costs a bomb in the US. Narayana has 18,000 beds across India, and if you ask most middle-class people in Bangalore about it, they'll speak highly of it.

There was one thing I kept thinking about over and over again after meeting him.

Narayana's market cap is around ₹38,000 crore. Now compare that to pretty much any half-decent financial services business in India, and it'll be valued more than that, including Zerodha. A brokerage, worth more than a hospital chain, that has probably saved hundreds of thousands of lives.

I get the arguments. If you're a fund manager/analyst, you can immediately explain it away using margins, capex, asset-light vs asset-heavy, and all that, and I'm not saying the market is wrong.

But it's still a strange world we've built, where the businesses closest to money get valued the highest, and the ones doing the hard and essential things get priced like boring utilities. A hospital carries physical infrastructure, enormous liability, thin margins and the actual weight of keeping people alive. And somehow that's worth less than a platform for buying and selling stocks.

I don't have a clean take on this. All of this just felt odd.

Ps: Nothing here is investment advice. For that, go to @zerodhavarsity

items.title: RT Ricardo: In 19 days, a jury in Oakland is going to decide whether the entire legal foundation of the AI industry is built on fraud. Everyone thinks...
items.description_html:
RT Ricardo
In 19 days, a jury in Oakland is going to decide whether the entire legal foundation of the AI industry is built on fraud.

Everyone thinks the Musk vs Altman lawsuit is a billionaire grudge match.

Two egos, one grudge, a $150 billion damages number designed for headlines.

Easy to dismiss. Easy to scroll past.

That's exactly what Altman wants you to think.

Because what's actually on trial on April 27 is something much BIGGER than Elon's hurt feelings...

A jury is going to decide whether you can legally take billions of dollars in nonprofit donations, use them to build the most valuable technology in human history, and then quietly convert that nonprofit into a for-profit company worth $850 billion.

If the answer is no, the entire AI industry has a problem.

Because OpenAI is not the only company that did this:

Anthropic was founded by OpenAI defectors using the same nonprofit-first mission language.

xAI pitches itself as building AI "for humanity."

Every frontier lab has used the moral cover of "we're doing this for the good of the world" to attract talent, capital, and regulatory goodwill they would have never gotten otherwise.

An Elon win doesn't just touch OpenAI. It creates a legal precedent that every AI company built on a nonprofit or public benefit promise becomes vulnerable to shareholder and donor clawback suits.

That's why this case matters. And that's why Altman is panicking.

Just look at what he did this week:

Elon filed a motion demanding the court remove Altman and Brockman from their roles and FORCE OpenAI to return to its nonprofit origins.

Then he amended the suit to say if he wins the $150 billion, all of it goes to OpenAI's charity arm. Not him. Zero dollars to Elon personally.

That amendment was surgical. It stripped Altman of his entire public defense.

He can no longer claim this is about Elon's ego or Elon's bank account. Elon is now legally on record saying he just wants the mission back.

OpenAI's response was to panic-write a letter to the California and Delaware attorneys general asking them to investigate Elon for "anti-competitive behavior." Their strategy chief publicly accused Elon of coordinating attacks with Mark Zuckerberg.

They called the lawsuit "harassment driven by ego and jealousy."

That's NOT the response of a company that thinks it's going to win.

Real companies with real defenses don't ask the government to silence the person suing them 3 weeks before trial. They let the evidence speak.

OpenAI is scrambling because they know what's in discovery.
Elon's team has been building this case for two years. Emails, board minutes, internal conversations about the conversion.

The kind of paper trail that juries understand and executives can't explain away.

And the timing couldn't be worse...

OpenAI is trying to IPO at $852 billion. They just raised $122 billion. Microsoft has $135 billion of exposure to them.

A jury verdict that even partially sides with Elon in late April or May would crater the entire IPO runway and send shockwaves through every major AI investor on Earth.

This is why Altman spent the last 2 weeks doing press tours and policy blueprints and "super intelligence agendas" aimed at Washington. He's trying to REFRAME himself as the responsible statesman of AI right before a jury decides if he's a con artist.

Most people will watch this trial start and think it's celebrity drama.

The smart money is watching it and realizing that the legal foundation of the AI boom is about to be tested in court for the first time EVER.

And if that foundation cracks, everything built on top of it is at risk.

items.description_html:
RT Mohamed A. El-Erian
While the debate continues over exactly what was agreed between the US and Iran regarding the Strait during the ceasefire, ask yourself the following question: What would we do if we were ship owners operating there?
I suspect—and please correct me if you disagree—that we would be far more inclined to get our equipment and colleagues out of there than to send them in. Ten ships out for every one or two in?
This, of course, highlights a larger point: the restoration of normal shipping activity through the Strait is not solely a decision for governments, nor is it something that switches on and off flawlessly.
#economy #markets #middleeastwar #StraitOfHurmoz

items.title: One of the industries that is growing rapidly due to renewable energy is HVDC- Why HVDC is Critical for Renewables: Long-Distance Transmission: Ideal ...
items.description_html:
One of the industries that is growing rapidly due to renewable energy is HVDC-

Why HVDC is Critical for Renewables:

Long-Distance Transmission: Ideal for connecting remote renewable sources (like offshore wind or desert solar) to cities with minimal power loss over long distances or undersea.

Grid Stability & Intermittency: HVDC provides superior control over power flow, allowing operators to stabilize voltage and frequency fluctuations caused by intermittent renewable sources.

Connecting Offshore Wind: HVDC is the standard for bringing offshore wind energy to shore efficiently using submarine cables.

Integrating Different Grids: It can link asynchronous power grids (running at different frequencies), which helps in trading renewable energy across regions.

Lower Transmission Costs & Infrastructure: Requires fewer conductor cables than AC, lowering material costs and reducing environmental footprint for long-distance bulk power transfer.

The Indian HVDC transmission market is experiencing rapid growth, valued at USD 3.86 billion in 2025 and projected to reach USD 5.84–6.31 billion by 2030-2031, expanding at a CAGR of 8.55%–12.6%. Driven by 500 GW renewable energy targets and long-distance power transfer needs

items.title: The Story of E 2-wheeler Sales in India - - Ola's market share has gone down to 12% from 35% in 2 years - Startup → Legacy shift is complete; Trust +...
items.description_html:
The Story of E 2-wheeler Sales in India -

- Ola's market share has gone down to 12% from 35% in 2 years
- Startup → Legacy shift is complete; Trust + distribution advantage is winning
- Ola had the first mover advantage but lost due to service backlog, perception issues, and reliability
- E 2W market expanded from 11L units to 13.5L units (FY25 to FY26)
- Ather is holding ground thanks to product development, feedback, and calculated growth

Sales Figures -

- 22% YoY growth in E-2W sales - 13.5L units sold in FY26 v/s 11.1L units in FY25
- Ola - sold only 10,118 vehicles in March 2026, a 5.4% market share. This is a sharp decline from the 22.1% share in April 2025. For FY26, Ola sold only 164,000 vehicles, less than 50% of the 344,000 units sold in FY25.
- The market is now dominated by legacy players: Bajaj Auto and TVS Motor (50% share almost)
- TVS Motor, sold 49,484 units in April 2026 (2x YoY)

- The 2-week pause is subject to Iran agreeing to the "complete, immediate, and safe opening" of the Strait of Hormuz

- ⁠RBI monetary policy also today
- Trump says the US and Iran are "far along with a definitive agreement for long-term peace."

items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
Happy to see my work in @thewire_in

GST’s key feature: it’s self-enforcing. Firms need invoices to claim credits → they demand compliance from suppliers → formalisation cascades through supply chains.

https://thewire.in/economy/how-the-gst-created-a-formalisation-cascade

from @Ideas4India

Paper
https://meghapatnaik.github.io/research/gst_prowess.pdf

items.title: Have been reading about this for a while now. India has a specifically important need for Fast Breeders. The control of uranium is with other countrie...
items.description_html:
Have been reading about this for a while now. India has a specifically important need for Fast Breeders. The control of uranium is with other countries and India has too little - and what it has, the real fissile stuff (U235) is way too little. We get more U238 - an isotope that isn't fissile by itself so in an FBR, it's combined with plutonium (Pu239) which produces energy + more Pu 239.

The energy is good for usage. The Pu produced is put back in as input - and when enough's there, you can use Thorium alongside and produce U-233. The idea of criticality is when the reaction is stable and producing enough neutrons to sustain fission but not too much or too fast.

The idea then is we can use U238, which is relatively more abundant, to produce plutonium (input fuel for FBRs) and also to produce U233 which is useful along with Thorium in the next stage of reactors which are more efficient energy producers.

For india, this means we can use our thorium reserves with relatively less uranium, and the more abundant form of uranium, to get more nuclear energy. India has among the highest thorium reserves in the world but very little uranium, and having other countries as a source of uranium is a bad idea (as we can see, every one is for themselves these days)

It will take another 20+ years to get there. But remember, this plan was set up in 1954, so it's been a long time already. It will be useful for India to get way more energy without having to rely on external sources, and the nuclear path is a good way ahead. The FBR breakthrough was needed, now I think we might need to wait for 5+ years to get enough U 233 that will help in the next stage. Nothing's sudden until it actually happens.

items.title: Kalyan, Senco and PNGS Reva all dropped Q4 updates and the headlines look epic but there is one thing interesting here - Kalyan India +65%, Senco +46%...
items.description_html:
Kalyan, Senco and PNGS Reva all dropped Q4 updates and the headlines look epic but there is one thing interesting here - Kalyan India +65%, Senco +46%, PNGS Reva +139%

Here's the problem -

Senco in their press release mentioned average realisation went from ₹84,782/10g in Q4FY25 to ₹1,51,783/10g in Q4FY26, +79% YoY, with another +20% on top QoQ. Assuming that should be true approximately for all the other players and run that math against the SSSG prints which inverts the picture. Senco's 34% SSSG on 79% gold means same-store gram volumes are down ~25%. Kalyan's vaunted 45% India SSSG? Volumes down ~19%.

Most of what you see is value gains rather than volume gains

Seeing this Senco launched 9k gold collection called Cloud 9 - 37.5% purity vs 22k's 91.6%, a roughly 60% cut in gold content per piece.

You don't engineer a structurally cheaper SKU in the same quarter you're reporting +46% growth unless you've privately concluded the consumer can't follow gold any higher. Kalyan is making the same bet through a different door they opened 14 Candere stores vs 28 Kalyan stores in Q4, and Candere (digital-first, diamond-led, lightweight) is now 124 stores against Kalyan India's 342, ~27% of the network. Candere did +360% in Q4 off a small base. Both companies are quietly hedging the 22k wedding-store format with affordability formats

Senco's FY27 guidance closes the loop. They've guided 20–25% value growth after delivering ~35% in FY26, with EBITDA margin held flat at 7.5–7.8% as operating leverage would be difficult with just value growth in place. Meanwhile Kalyan's ME segment grew 39% (volume down ~22% by the same gold math) with Israel-Iran spillover hitting walk-ins for three of four weeks in March and building material disruption delaying new showroom launches; international is 11% of revenue but a higher share of profit but if the issue is elongated than it can be a pain on international EBITDA. The Akshaya Tritiya tell is that both Kalyan and Senco called advance bookings "encouraging"

Disclaimer - Not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: On a humid morning in Fukuoka, a coastal city in southern Japan, a new kind of power came online. Japan has launched Asia’s first osmotic power plant...
items.description_html:
On a humid morning in Fukuoka, a coastal city in southern Japan, a new kind of power came online. Japan has launched Asia’s first osmotic power plant, which generates electricity by mixing fresh water with salt water.

“It’s a meaningful plan—the start of a plan, perhaps—in our response against climate change,” said Kenji Hirokawa, director of the Seawater Desalination Center, which runs the facility, as per Gizmodo.

Fukuoka’s plant is only the second of its kind worldwide, following one in Denmark that opened in 2023. Japan’s version is larger and marks a step forward for this little-used but promising renewable energy source.

The plant will generate about 880,000 kilowatt hours of electricity per year—enough to help run a nearby desalination facility and supply around 220 homes. That equals the output of two soccer fields of solar panels, but osmotic power keeps running day and night, in any weather

items.title: ET Article - Mangalore, Bhopal & Aurangabad Step Up to Answer the Call of GCCs; Tier-2 cities now make up 30–32% of all new job postings, despite slo...
items.description_html:
ET Article -

Mangalore, Bhopal & Aurangabad Step Up to Answer the Call of GCCs; Tier-2 cities now make up 30–32% of all new job postings, despite slower growth

India’s global capability centres (GCCs) are expanding beyond traditional hubs, with the first set of Tier-2 cities such as Mangalore, Bhopal, and Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar emerging as key locations for new tech hubs, driven by cost advantage and access to talent

While GCCs' move into smaller cities such as Coimbatore, Indore, and Lucknow is well documented, more centres are coming up as states push policies and build infrastructure to attract such investments

Tier-2 cities now make up 30–32% of all new job postings, according to data.

Hiring demand for tech and GCC roles in cities like Mangalore and Indore has picked up notably, driven by the rise of nano-GCCs. Demand for such locations has risen to ~35% of new evaluations in FY25, up from 14.9% in FY24

Key Drivers

Cost arbitrage vs Tier-1 cities
Access to untapped talent pools
State-level incentives & infrastructure push
AI-led distributed work models

no reco

items.title: Nearly 95% of Indian households have access to basic drinking water, a rate of progress that has surpassed the world's average. Tap or piped water is ...
items.description_html:
Nearly 95% of Indian households have access to basic drinking water, a rate of progress that has surpassed the world's average. Tap or piped water is the single largest source of drinking water for urban Indians.

The most recent data on access to drinking water in India comes from the 79th round of the National Sample Survey (NSS), conducted in 2022-23.

Nearly seven in ten households across urban India use tap water as their principal source of drinking water. The principal source refers to the one from which most of the drinking water was obtained by the household during the last 365 days.

However, there are some key peculiarities at the state level. Bottled water (which includes home-delivered cans of water) has become an important source of drinking water in the southern states.

In urban Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu and Telangana, over a quarter of households get their drinking water primarily from bottled water. In Kerala, on the other hand, household wells are the most important source of drinking water.

Driven by the southern states, bottled water has become a key source of drinking water in urban India, now serving 15% of households nationally.

How does bottled water use compare in rural India? Read @Rukmini's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-drinking-water/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Water #WASH #WashData #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT Vasant Shetty | Building Mundhe Banni: Our next story is about the Rocky Bhai of the seafood business. A boy from Mysore, with absolutely no backgr...
items.description_html:
RT Vasant Shetty | Building Mundhe Banni
Our next story is about the Rocky Bhai of the seafood business.

A boy from Mysore, with absolutely no background in seafood, is today running a global seafood company. From the Atlantic to the Pacific, from the Arabian Sea to the Indian Ocean, his company operates across oceans, runs frozen food processing factories in multiple European countries, has offices in more than 10 countries, and sells fish across the world, generating Rs 10,000 crore in revenue.

And here is the surprising part. He comes from a very humble, middle class background in Mysore. You cannot get a more rooted Mysorean Kannadiga than @ksutham.

What he has built is a truly fascinating story. He is also an incredible storyteller. We often hear that Kannadigas are less ambitious, that they are content with what they have. This story is 180 degrees opposite to that belief. Here is someone with the ambition to conquer the world and build the largest seafood company on the planet.

This is a story for anyone who feels limited by where they come from. You did not go to IIT. You did not have wealthy parents. You did not have constant guidance. You did not have generational wealth. You did not come from a business background. None of that has to be an impediment.

If you have the clarity to know what you want, the willingness to figure things out, and the mindset to ask what is the worst that can happen, you can build something extraordinary.

This is a story we truly, truly enjoyed producing. The trailer drops today, and the episode goes live this Friday, the 10th.

@Shishir_S_U @mundhebanni


Vasant Shetty | Building Mundhe Banni: With the new clue, many got it right..🙌

Our next guest is @ksutham, founder of Captain Fresh. He is literally the Rocky Bhai of sea food business.

Absolutely roller coaster ride...

Trailer loading.. Episode on 10th April..

items.title: P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/E...
items.description_html:
P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/EBITDA. I had demonstrated this through correlation analysis in an earlier investor memo.

The chart below tracks the P/B ratio (blue line) of the Nifty Smallcap 100 Index against the 25th percentile of its trailing 5-year P/B range (orange line). In simple terms, when the blue line falls below the orange line, the current valuation is in the lowest quartile of the past five years.

Since P/B is broadly proportional to ROE, the structural improvement in index ROE—from ~8–12% in the previous decade to ~15% currently—has led to a gradual upward shift in the orange line over time.

Notably, for the first time since the COVID crash, the current P/B has moved below this 25th percentile threshold. Historically, similar instances—during the 2011–2013 downturn, Q1 2016 correction, and the COVID crash—have been followed by strong returns over the subsequent 1–3 years.

This does not imply that markets have bottomed, nor does it guarantee short-term gains. In an event-driven environment, especially with geopolitical risks such as an escalation in war, markets could decline further by 10–20%.

However, what can be said with a reasonably high degree of confidence is that forward-looking returns over a 1–3 year horizon from such valuation levels have historically been better than average.

items.title: Mr Ramesh Damani on how his father was one of the Earliest Value investors - “I was born in the city of Hyderabad, but fairly early on in my life, my...
items.description_html:
Mr Ramesh Damani on how his father was one of the Earliest Value investors -

“I was born in the city of Hyderabad, but fairly early on in my life, my parents moved to Mumbai. Even in those days, which were the early 1960s, some of my fondest memories were of my father's involvement in the stock exchange

The residence where I live today, and even then, is just across the stock exchange. My father, Mr. Shrichand Damani, started in the stock market in the 1960s, when it was very small. There was an influx of stock market people then. A lot of old stock market hands lived in my building.

I remember when I went to school, my father was the only one in the stock market. Most of the students had their fathers working in MNCs or running their own business. My father’s profession puzzled me a lot. What was it that my father did? Then the market was extremely small, so there wasn’t much money to be made.

Early years were a struggle for the family. But within that subset of investors - and I know because I met his friends later - my dad was considered a better-informed investor of his era. This was primarily because he was a voracious reader, a habit that he passed on to me. He inculcated the habit of reading the international press very early in me.

So at a time when companies like Nestle, Siemens, etc. were coming to India, he was already familiar with them. That gave him a leg up over his contemporaries, who only knew Tata Steel or Tata Motors, and didn’t know what MNCs or globalization would mean.

I remember him speaking very fondly in the 1960s about companies like Specialty Foods (which later became Nestle) and ABB. So he was amongst the early value investors who bought MNC Stocks”

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

items.title: RT cecfee: Heat’s impact on students is well documented. But what about early childhood, when cognitive foundations are formed? A new study by Bensto...
items.description_html:
RT cecfee
Heat’s impact on students is well documented. But what about early childhood, when cognitive foundations are formed?

A new study by Benston John, E. Somanathan and Rohini Somanathan shows that a simple intervention -painting anganwadi roofs white - reduced indoor heat and improved children’s cognitive performance by 6–7%.

In a new article of @Ideas4India, the authors argue that findings from this study are striking, not just because of the learning gains, but because it comes from a low-cost intervention (around ₹1 per child per day) that requires no ongoing energy use.

Read the article: https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/human-development/cooling-classrooms-to-protect-human-capital-evidence-from-keralas-anganwadis

Link to the study: https://www.isid.ac.in/~cecfee/publication/when-the-roof-reflects-heat-learning-and-adaptation-in-early-childhood-settings/

@emc_kerala | Environment for Development

items.title: FIIs sold ₹1.6 lakh crores in 2025 and ₹1.1 lakh crores in just the first 3 months of 2026 Rs 2.7 Lakh cr of investments exited India in just 15 mon...
items.description_html:
FIIs sold ₹1.6 lakh crores in 2025 and ₹1.1 lakh crores in just the first 3 months of 2026

Rs 2.7 Lakh cr of investments exited India in just 15 months

If SIP Book hadn't supported the DIIs, our markets would have been under the ground

Requesting the Government to reward equity investors through -

1 - LTCG/STCG tax reduction + Capital Gains Tax Stability Framework - Commit to a 5–10 year tax roadmap for equities (no surprises).
2 - Conversation with FIIs to bring them back (cut down on the operational hassles and taxation issues)
3 - Introduce Tax deductions for equity SIPs (like 80C-lite category)
4- Position India as “China+1 capital market” or play on the Geopolitical neutral image
5 - Push settlement of trades/investments in INR globally - Reduces FX risk dependency
6 - Create a Sovereign Wealth fund via LIC and other big players in the war Footing
7 - Build more on Mutual Fund Sahi hain Campaign - More Campaigns can be created, such as Equity investments Sahi hain, har mahine Stock Investment Yojana, and so on

Also, we need to stabilize our currency, move away from massive imports in Oil, Fertilizers, and Semiconductors (thanks to EVs/CNG and other platforms in Mobility and Grid movement towards renewables, we are taking the right steps)

Fingers crossed!

items.description_html:
Dmart's Q4FY26 update shows that it has reached 500 stores

DMart opened its very first store in 2002 in Powai, Mumbai

24 years to reach 500 stores! Just 20 stores per year and around 2 stores per month/1 store per 14 days

Dmart has always been cautious about opening new stores - Slow and steady does win the race!

But make no mistake, their stores are Huge - DMart stores typically average around 30,000–40,000 sq. ft., with a recent shift towards larger "hypermarket-style" stores often reaching 50,000–60,000 sq. ft and these are mostly owned properties

We all know about the other retailer that went berserk on store additions and eventually went bankrupt due to an overload of debt

items.title: Unless you build, you don't learn. If you are using Codex or Claude Code or even Git agents, always have two files: 1. Architecture md: describes ever...
items.description_html:
Unless you build, you don't learn. If you are using Codex or Claude Code or even Git agents, always have two files:
1. Architecture md: describes every architecture decision, what to build & the sequence of build. If you close VSCode & there is no session memory, this file is your savior.
2. Decisions md: make sure your codegen write every decision it made into a decisions markdown. Ask it to also record why it selected something over alternative. This gives you way more learning than anything else.
Ensure you keep updating architecture md so that you don't need a separate State md file.

items.title: FY26 was a year of broad-based recovery turning into selective outperformance. The sector ended March with every segment in green —> PVs +8.4%, 2Ws +...
items.description_html:
FY26 was a year of broad-based recovery turning into selective outperformance. The sector ended March with every segment in green —> PVs +8.4%, 2Ws +12.5%, CVs +12.5%, Tractors +18.5% (retail).

Growth is great but the real thing in OEM has been the shift in MS which one should look for -

> In terms of shift in MS :- Winners: M&M (PV+Tractor), TVS (2W+EV+3W+Export), Tata (PV+CV), Ather (E-2W); Losers: Maruti (PV share), Hyundai (PV volumes), Ola Electric (E-2W), Honda 2W (strategic drift)

> E-2W at 9.8% (Mar26) :- expect double-digit in FY27. E-PV at 5% — this is where E-2W was 18 months ago. The ancillary supply chain implications: motor controllers, BMS, wiring harnesses, thermal management all might see volume inflection.

> CNG is 23.6% of PV retail in Mar26. This benefits :- gas distribution companies (IGL, MGL, Gujarat Gas), CNG kit suppliers, and dual-fuel engine component makers. The CNG-to-EV transition in PVs is a 5-7 year bridge

> Also interestingly inventory cycle might be your friend now as post this fast growth period the dealer inventory is at 28 days now which is low. No channel correction risk in the near term. Watch for any build-up above 40 days

> Crude oil and input cost volatility from Middle East tensions could compress OEM margins. Planned price hikes by OEMs in Q1FY27 could slow demand if consumer sentiment weakens.

Disclaimer - Not a recommendation to buy and sell

items.description_html:
RT Pranay Kotasthane
Details of India's semiconductor manufacturing projects are scattered across hundreds of press releases, news reports, and government announcements. There's also a lot of hype and misreporting. So I built a tracker using Claude Code to fix this. It scores each project on technical complexity (benchmarked against a 3nm fab), tracks slippage from original announced dates, and links every data point to its source.

It's open source. If you have a status update, a correction, or know about a new facility, there's a form to contribute. Let's build this together.
